advaita.rendering: Among royal chaplains (purohitas), know Me as Brhaspati — the foremost, since he is the chief priest of Indra, who is sovereign among rulers. Among commanders of armies, I am Skanda, the general of the divine hosts. Among bodies of water, the still lake-pools born of divine excavation — I am the ocean (sagara).
advaita.anchor: Sankara specifies that Brhaspati qualifies as mukhya because his patron is Indra, mukhya among kings; Skanda is devasenapati; and sagara is glossed as devakhatani saramsi — divine-dug reservoirs, not rivers. The identifications exhaust their categories; Brahman as inner controller is manifest as the excellence in each domain.
advaita.divergence: For Sankara these are illustrations of vibhuti — the single Brahman appearing as graduated excellence. No devotional relation is implied; the verse is epistemological, showing the student where to locate the non-dual ground.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Among priests, the eminent Brhaspati — that one am I. Among army-commanders, Skanda am I. Among bodies of water, the ocean am I.
viśiṣṭādvaita.anchor: Ramanuja's commentary is characteristically concise here, establishing only the uptkrsta (eminent) identification for Brhaspati; the verse is part of the Bhagavan's own self-disclosure in the vibhuti-yoga, demonstrating that supremacy in any domain is His immanent presence within the world-body (jagat-sarira).
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence: For Ramanuja, these identifications disclose not abstract Brahman but the personal Bhagavan who is inseparable from yet distinct from his cit-acit modes. Devotion (bhakti) to the lord is the appropriate response; the vibhutis are invitations to upasana, not dissolving the distinctions between Isvara and world.

dvaita.rendering: Among chaplains Brhaspati, among generals Skanda, among waters the ocean — in each, the supremacy is Hari's own, since all excellence in dependent beings flows only from His independent will.
dvaita.anchor: NOTE: Madhvacarya did not comment on this sloka per the supplied payload. This rendering is constructed from Madhva's established dvaita hermeneutic applied to parallel vibhuti verses (10.20-10.42): every superlative names a jiva whose special excellence is a granted reflection of Vishnu's svatantrya (absolute independence); the named entities are real and eternally distinct, never merged.
dvaita.divergence: Madhva would insist that Brhaspati, Skanda, and the ocean remain real separate entities — not mere appearances of Brahman. Their pre-eminence is Vishnu's gift, not an identity. This is the sharpest divergence from Advaita.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Among chaplains I am Brhaspati; among still bodies of water (sthira-jalasayananam) — those that are steady, not restlessly flowing — I am the ocean.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Vallabha's commentary singles out the qualification 'sthira-jalasayanam' — still, stable reservoirs — for the sagara identification, distinguishing the ocean's nature as one of settled depth rather than turbulent motion. In Pustimarga, Krsna's lila permeates all excellence; the still-water gloss evokes the quality of prasada-rasa: the devotee receives grace as a still vessel receives depth.
śuddhādvaita.divergence: Vallabha's gloss on 'sarasam' as sthira-jalasayanam is unique in the panel — a qualitative-aesthetic note absent from Sankara and Sridhara. The verse's enumeration is received as a lyrical catalogue of Krsna's rasic presence, not a metaphysical argument.

bhakti.rendering: Among priests, know Me as Brhaspati — foremost because he is the divine chaplain (devapurohita). Among army-commanders (senaninas), I am Skanda the divine general (devasenapati). Among bodies of still water (sthira-jalasayanam), I am the ocean (samudra).
bhakti.anchor: Sridhara follows the three-limbed structure closely: devapurohitatvat ('by reason of being the god's own priest') is the explanatory phrase for Brhaspati; devasenapati is the gloss for Skanda; samudra (ocean) for sagara. The commentary is philological-devotional — it identifies the referents precisely, then allows the accumulated vibhutis to evoke Bhagavan's pervasive greatness for the devotee's heart.
bhakti.divergence: Sridhara's register is the most balanced in the panel — neither the metaphysical austerity of Sankara nor the rapturous lila-frame of Vallabha. His use of samudra (rather than Sankara's sagara with its sagar-etymological note) reflects a straightforward gloss tradition.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Because Indra is supreme among all kings, his chaplain Brhaspati is supreme among all royal priests — and that Brhaspati is Me. Among generals, I am Skanda — called Guha — the divine commander. Among bodies of divine-dug still water (devakhata-jalasayanam), I am the ocean — the great reservoir excavated by the sons of Sagara.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Madhusudana provides the richest etymological commentary in the panel: Indra's sovereignty grounds Brhaspati's primacy among purohitas; Skanda is identified additionally as Guha (the hidden, cave-born one); and sagara is glossed via the Puranic etymology — sagara-putraih khato jalasayo — the lake dug by Sagara's sons, giving sagara its name. This double-naming (Skanda/Guha, sagara/ocean-of-Sagara) is characteristic of Madhusudana's synthesis: the Advaita ground is shown through the devotional particularity of names.
advaita-bhakti.divergence: Madhusudana alone in this panel supplies the Puranic derivation of sagara, and alone names Skanda by his esoteric epithet Guha. This is the advaita-bhakti signature: jnana supplies the unified ground, bhakti supplies the name-by-name intimacy.

so_what_questions: Why does Krsna claim to be the chaplain (purohita) and not the king — what does this reveal about the relationship between wisdom-authority and political power in the Gita's world?

Skanda is the god of war and also called Guha ('the hidden one') — what is the significance of choosing a name that means both 'general' and 'secret' to represent divine excellence in martial affairs?

Brhaspati is the teacher of the gods, not merely their priest — does the verse's identification collapse the distinction between priestly function and teaching function, and what does that imply for one's own role as guide or advisor?

The ocean is identified as supreme among 'still bodies of water' (saras), not among rivers — why might depth and stillness be the chosen quality of divine excellence in water, and what does this suggest about the Gita's aesthetic of power?

Each named vibhuti (Brhaspati, Skanda, sagara) is the best of a category defined by function, not merely by size — how does this functional definition of supremacy differ from ranking by quantity or magnitude?
If I see Krsna in the commander of an army or in a king's wisest counselor, does that change how I regard human authority — does this verse provide a theology of legitimate leadership?
This verse names three domains — priestly, military, natural — in one breath. What does the speed and density of the enumeration suggest about the nature of vibhuti-yoga as a contemplative practice?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself in the role of advisor — the one whose counsel carries weight — recognize that the authority you exercise is not yours to own. The competence through which Brahman-as-Brhaspati speaks has no personal claimant. Act precisely, advise without ego-stake in outcome, and treat your own expertise as a borrowed instrument of the non-dual ground.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Every time you encounter someone whose guidance is genuinely excellent — a mentor, a doctor, a teacher whose word reliably illuminates — practice seeing them as a site of the Bhagavan's immanent presence in the world-body. This is not mere metaphor for Ramanuja; it is a bhakti-exercise: namaskara to the excellence, which is namaskara to its source.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any organization or community, the most capable leader is not self-made — their excellence is granted, not generated. Madhva's frame invites humility in the strong and trust in the weak: Hari's will is operating through the best among generals and advisors. Your own leadership is service rendered under delegation, and every success is to be returned as puja.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's still-water gloss offers a daily image for receiving grace: be the deep still reservoir, not the rushing river. In practice, before any high-stakes conversation or decision, pause into stillness. The ocean's supremacy is its depth and settledness, not its turbulence. Prasada enters the vessel that is not agitated.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's balanced register suggests a devotional naming-practice: in the morning, identify one 'Brhaspati' in your life — the advisor or teacher who serves the highest — and one 'Skanda' — the protector who guards what is vulnerable. Recognize Bhagavan in those roles and offer your respect to them as vibhuti-bearers, not merely as useful people.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's double-naming (Skanda-as-Guha, sagara-with-its-story) points to a practice of intimate learning about the names you love. The more precisely and richly you know a name — its etymology, its Puranic story, its epithet — the deeper the jnana-bhakti contact. Study one name of the divine today not to accumulate information but to deepen intimacy.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present; dvaita rendering flagged as constructed from hermeneutic (no Madhva commentary on this sloka); Vallabha's sthira-jalasayana gloss and Madhusudana's sagara-etymology and Guha-epithet preserved as genuine doctrinal divergences; no fake Sanskrit or HTML artifacts included.
